summary: DHH announces new Rails features that expand its domain language as an alternative to full-blown dependency injection. He introduces model, service, and observer declarations for controllers that replace explicit require statements and enable programmatic dependency introspection. He also adds automatic requires for Active Record associations and a simple mock system using Ruby's open classes. These lightweight additions, created with minimal code, lead him to question whether a full DI framework like Needle is needed in Rails at all.
key_points: New domain keywords (model, service, observer) replace explicit require statements while enabling programmatic dependency introspection on controllers
Active Record associations now auto-require their related classes, eliminating redundant require statements
A simple test/mocks directory convention leverages Ruby's open classes to replace services with mocks without any DI framework
These minimal-code solutions cast doubt on whether full-blown dependency injection is needed in Rails
The philosophy is to shift repetitive burden from the developer to the framework while keeping conceptual complexity flat
money_quotes: This it not only shorter and more expressive, it also opens up to a world of interesting automation and scaffolding when you can programatically inquire about the dependencies of a controller.
Just like I was thrilled to be rid of the semi-colon at the end of each line when I ditched PHP for Ruby, this will further swift the burden of repetition over to the framework.
Ruby has open classes, you can reopen anything — even core classes!
It's often much nicer to rely on as much 'real' functionality as possible. Just mock out the few methods that would otherwise make testing hard to do.
These latest breakthroughs does cast some doubt in my mind on whether full-blown dependency injection is needed in Rails.
All of the additions above where created with an absolute minimal amount of code. They give zeroconf dependency injection for mocks and the conceptual complexity of Rails doesn't go up one bit.
I got my fix, so to speak.
key_insight: Simple, convention-based solutions that leverage Ruby's dynamic nature can solve the same problems as dependency injection frameworks while adding zero conceptual complexity to the framework.
